wrong.A: Surveillance alone for a 3cm non-polypoid lesion with high-grade dysplasia that cannot be endoscopically removed is inappropriate — it leaves unresectable, high-risk mucosa in situ with no intervention. High-grade dysplasia in this context represents the highest pre-malignant risk.
wrong.B: ESD is a valid technique for en-bloc resection of flat lesions ≤2–3cm at expert centres. However, for a 3cm lesion with a complex morphology (Paris 0-IIa + 0-IIb) that has already been assessed as not amenable to standard endoscopic resection, ESD may still be attempted at an ESD expert centre as an alternative to surgery. However, surgical resection avoids the risk of incomplete endoscopic removal and provides staging if invasive cancer is found.
wrong.D: TEM (Transanal Endoscopic Microsurgery) is a technique for transanal resection of rectal lesions. It is anatomically limited to the rectum (typically ≤15cm from the anal verge) and has no role in right colon lesions.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Colon Cancer v2.2024 — flat lesions not amenable to en-bloc endoscopic resection should undergo surgical resection; high-grade dysplasia is a standard indication for complete removal
explanation.trial: Kahi CJ (Gastrointest Endosc 2019): piecemeal EMR of large flat lesions — 16% residual/recurrent rate at first surveillance colonoscopy. Jayanna M (Dis Colon Rectum 2013): surgical resection for endoscopically unresectable large flat lesions — 18% had unsuspected invasive carcinoma on final histology.
explanation.pearl: When endoscopy cannot achieve en-bloc resection of a flat or sessile lesion, the question becomes: can ESD be safely performed at this centre? If yes, offer ESD as an alternative to surgery. If not, proceed to surgical resection. Never accept piecemeal resection for lesions where invasive cancer cannot be excluded — piecemeal margins cannot be assessed.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man undergoes elective open sigmoidectomy for diverticular disease. On day 5, he develops fever, WBC 18 × 10⁹/L, and watery diarrhoea. He was on prophylactic co-amoxiclav perioperatively. C. difficile toxin assay is positive. Oral metronidazole was commenced on day 4 with no improvement. What is the appropriate next step?

options.A: Continue metronidazole for 14 days — treatment requires at least 14 days to be effective
options.B: Switch to oral vancomycin 125mg QDS — first-line treatment for non-severe CDI where metronidazole fails
options.C: Commence IV metronidazole — oral absorption may be inadequate in post-operative ileus
options.D: Emergency colectomy — post-operative CDI always requires surgical management

explanation.correct: Clostridium difficile infection (CDI) treatment has been significantly revised following IDSA 2021 guidelines. Metronidazole is no longer first-line for CDI — vancomycin or fidaxomicin is preferred due to superior efficacy and lower recurrence rates. If the patient was started on metronidazole and is not improving, the appropriate switch is to oral vancomycin 125mg four times daily for 10–14 days (the IDSA/SHEA standard first-line therapy for non-severe CDI). Fidaxomicin 200mg BD is an alternative with lower recurrence rates. Metronidazole failure rates exceed 15% in hospitalised patients.

wrong.A: Continuing metronidazole after 4 days of no improvement is not appropriate — the 2021 IDSA guidelines no longer recommend metronidazole for CDI regardless of severity. Metronidazole has been de-escalated due to inferior cure rates (90% vs 96–98% for vancomycin) and higher recurrence rates.
wrong.C: IV metronidazole has poor luminal concentrations in the colon (it relies on colonic secretion) and is less effective than oral vancomycin for CDI. IV metronidazole may be added to oral vancomycin in fulminant CDI (shock, ileus), but it should not replace oral vancomycin as the primary anticolitis agent.
wrong.D: Emergency colectomy is indicated only for fulminant CDI with toxic megacolon (colonic diameter >6cm on CT), bowel perforation, or haemodynamic deterioration despite maximal medical therapy. This patient has non-severe CDI — surgical management at this stage is premature and inappropriate.

explanation.guideline: IDSA/SHEA CDI Clinical Practice Guidelines 2021 — vancomycin 125mg QDS (10–14 days) or fidaxomicin 200mg BD is first-line for non-severe CDI; metronidazole no longer recommended as first-line
explanation.trial: Johnson S et al. (Clin Infect Dis 2021): IDSA update — vancomycin and fidaxomicin superior to metronidazole in cure rates and recurrence prevention. Fidaxomicin associated with 40% lower recurrence rate than vancomycin (CLARITY, DIFICID trials).
explanation.pearl: Define CDI severity for treatment selection: non-severe (WBC <15, creatinine <1.5× baseline) → vancomycin or fidaxomicin; severe (WBC ≥15, creatinine >1.5×) → vancomycin 125mg QDS; fulminant (shock, ileus, megacolon) → vancomycin 500mg QDS + IV metronidazole 500mg TDS ± surgery.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old woman with ECOG 2 performance status, controlled hypertension, and mild cognitive impairment presents with a sigmoid adenocarcinoma (pT3 N1a M0) after elective laparoscopic sigmoidectomy. Pathology reports 14 lymph nodes, 1 positive, R0 resection. She tolerates the surgery well and is discharged on day 5. At the post-operative MDT, adjuvant chemotherapy is discussed. What is the most appropriate recommendation?

options.A: FOLFOX (oxaliplatin + 5-FU + leucovorin) for 12 cycles — identical to younger patients with stage III disease
options.B: Capecitabine monotherapy — single-agent fluoropyrimidine is appropriate first-line adjuvant therapy for fit elderly patients; oxaliplatin adds toxicity without proven OS benefit in patients >70 years
options.C: No adjuvant chemotherapy — age >75 and ECOG 2 are absolute contraindications to adjuvant therapy
options.D: CAPOX for 8 cycles — reduced intensity combination regimen is appropriate for all elderly stage III patients

explanation.correct: For elderly patients (>70 years) with stage III colon cancer, the benefit of adding oxaliplatin to adjuvant fluoropyrimidine therapy is not clearly established. Multiple subgroup analyses of MOSAIC (FOLFOX) and the ACCENT database demonstrate that the DFS benefit of oxaliplatin is attenuated in patients >70 years, while peripheral neuropathy risk (cumulative, irreversible) is substantially higher. The NCCN and ESMO guidelines recommend capecitabine monotherapy as a standard adjuvant option for elderly patients where oxaliplatin toxicity outweighs benefit. ECOG 2 further limits tolerance for combination chemotherapy. A geriatric assessment (G8 or CGA) should guide the final decision.

wrong.A: Full-dose FOLFOX for an ECOG 2, 76-year-old patient without geriatric assessment is inappropriate. Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy is dose-dependent, cumulative, and irreversible — a 76-year-old with ECOG 2 has high risk of dose-limiting toxicity and functional deterioration with full oxaliplatin-based therapy.
wrong.C: Age >75 and ECOG 2 are not absolute contraindications to adjuvant therapy. Functional status, comorbidity burden, and patient preference all inform the decision. Stage IIIA N1a disease has a meaningful survival benefit from adjuvant fluoropyrimidine therapy, and a fit 76-year-old can safely receive capecitabine.
wrong.D: CAPOX (8 cycles = 6 months) may be appropriate for younger, fitter elderly patients, but ECOG 2 and cognitive impairment in this patient favour single-agent capecitabine over combination therapy. Reduce intensity before reducing duration.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Colon Cancer v2.2024 — capecitabine acceptable as adjuvant therapy for stage III in elderly patients; geriatric assessment recommended before adjuvant decisions in patients >70 years
explanation.trial: MOSAIC subgroup analysis (André, J Clin Oncol 2009): no significant DFS benefit from FOLFOX over LV5FU2 in patients >70 years; OS not improved. ACCENT database meta-analysis: oxaliplatin benefit significantly attenuated in >70s.
explanation.pearl: Use the G8 frailty screening tool (8-item questionnaire, score ≤14 indicates frailty needing comprehensive geriatric assessment) before offering adjuvant chemotherapy to patients >70 years. Frail patients have reduced treatment tolerance and may derive less net benefit from chemotherapy than non-frail patients.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman undergoes laparoscopic left hemicolectomy with primary anastomosis and no diverting stoma for a descending colon cancer. On day 7, she develops severe abdominal pain, guarding, and fever 39.2°C. CT abdomen shows free peritoneal air and faecal material diffusely in the abdomen. She is haemodynamically unstable (BP 85/50 mmHg, HR 115 bpm) despite initial resuscitation. What is the most appropriate definitive surgical management?

options.A: CT-guided percutaneous drainage and broad-spectrum antibiotics — Grade B anastomotic leak
options.B: Emergency return to theatre: laparotomy, washout, Hartmann's procedure (anastomosis takedown, end colostomy)
options.C: Laparoscopic repeat anastomosis with defunctioning ileostomy
options.D: Endoscopic vacuum therapy (EVT) placement with ICU admission

explanation.correct: This patient has a Grade C anastomotic leak (free peritoneal contamination, faecal peritonitis, haemodynamic instability) requiring emergency laparotomy. The Hartmann's procedure — anastomosis takedown, proximal end colostomy, and closure of the rectal/colonic stump — is the standard management for unstable patients with faecal peritonitis. The principles are: (1) source control (remove the leaking anastomosis); (2) peritoneal lavage (copious washout); (3) avoid re-anastomosis in a contaminated, shocked patient; (4) resuscitate in ICU. Hartmann's reversal can be planned 3–6 months later when the patient has recovered and nutritional status is restored.

wrong.A: CT-guided drainage is appropriate for Grade B leaks (contained pericolic collection without free peritoneal contamination, haemodynamically stable). Free intraperitoneal faecal matter and haemodynamic instability define Grade C — this requires operative intervention, not percutaneous drainage.
wrong.C: Re-anastomosis in a contaminated field with faecal peritonitis in a haemodynamically unstable patient is contraindicated — it will inevitably fail and add to the morbidity. Damage control surgery (Hartmann's) is the principle.
wrong.D: Endoscopic vacuum therapy is appropriate for contained presacral cavities after low anterior resection (Grade B leaks after LAR). It cannot address free intraperitoneal faecal contamination or haemodynamic instability — this patient requires emergency laparotomy, not endoscopy.

explanation.guideline: International Study Group of Rectal Cancer — Grade C anastomotic leak: emergency laparotomy, source control, peritoneal washout, Hartmann's procedure or defunctioning for unstable patients
explanation.trial: Hirst NA et al. (Ann Surg 2020): Grade C anastomotic leak mortality 15–20% without surgical intervention; Hartmann's procedure reduces mortality compared to non-operative management in unstable patients. Damage control laparotomy principles applied.
explanation.pearl: In the unstable patient with faecal peritonitis after anastomotic leak: do not attempt to save the anastomosis. The priority is haemorrhage control and source control — Hartmann's in 90 minutes with good washout is better than a 3-hour attempt at re-anastomosis. Reversal of Hartmann's can be offered electively.

questions.stem: A 49-year-old man undergoes genetic testing after his father died of colorectal cancer at age 42, and his sister was diagnosed with endometrial cancer at age 38. His test reveals a pathogenic MLH1 germline mutation (Lynch syndrome). He has no current symptoms and a recent colonoscopy 18 months ago was normal. What is the most appropriate colorectal surveillance recommendation for this patient?

options.A: Colonoscopy every 5 years — Lynch syndrome carries only modest lifetime cancer risk
options.B: Colonoscopy every 1–2 years beginning at age 20–25 or 2–5 years before the earliest family diagnosis
options.C: Prophylactic total colectomy now — all Lynch syndrome carriers should undergo prophylactic surgery
options.D: CT colonography every 3 years — equivalent to colonoscopy for Lynch syndrome surveillance

explanation.correct: Lynch syndrome (HNPCC) caused by MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, or PMS2 germline mutations carries a 40–70% lifetime risk of colorectal cancer (MLH1/MSH2 carriers have the highest risk). Annual or biennial colonoscopy beginning at age 20–25 (or 2–5 years before the earliest age of CRC diagnosis in the family) is the NCCN and ESMO standard surveillance recommendation. Colonoscopy is preferred over CT colonography because Lynch syndrome CRCs may develop from flat or subtle adenomas that are detectable only by high-definition endoscopy. Chromoendoscopy increases polyp detection in Lynch syndrome surveillance.

wrong.A: Five-year intervals are inappropriate for Lynch syndrome. The adenoma-to-carcinoma sequence is accelerated in MMR-deficient mucosa — polyps can progress to cancer in as little as 2–3 years, compared to 10+ years in average-risk individuals. Annual or biennial colonoscopy is required.
wrong.C: Prophylactic total colectomy is not recommended for Lynch syndrome carriers without established cancer. It is discussed when the patient develops a first colorectal cancer (at which point subtotal colectomy is often preferred over segmental resection to avoid the high risk of metachronous CRC). Prophylactic colectomy in a gene carrier without cancer is not the standard recommendation.
wrong.D: CT colonography (virtual colonoscopy) has insufficient sensitivity for flat lesions and smaller polyps and cannot allow polypectomy. It is not an appropriate substitute for optical colonoscopy in Lynch syndrome surveillance where rapid adenoma development and subtle lesions require direct mucosal assessment.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Colorectal Cancer Screening v2.2024 — MLH1/MSH2 Lynch syndrome: colonoscopy every 1–2 years starting at age 20–25; MSH6/PMS2: colonoscopy every 1–2 years starting at age 25–30
explanation.trial: CAPP2 trial (Burn, Lancet 2011): aspirin 600mg daily reduced CRC incidence in Lynch syndrome by 63% at 4.5-year follow-up (post hoc analysis). NCCN notes aspirin can be discussed with Lynch syndrome patients as chemoprevention, although dose and duration remain under study.
explanation.pearl: Lynch syndrome cancer spectrum (Amsterdam criteria II — '3-2-1-0 rule'): 3 relatives with Lynch-associated cancer; 2 successive generations; 1 affected before age 50; 0 patients with FAP excluded. Extra-colonic Lynch cancers: endometrial (highest risk after CRC), ovarian, gastric, urinary tract, small bowel, biliary, brain.

questions.stem: A 53-year-old man has a biopsy-confirmed T2 N0 low rectal cancer at 3cm from the anal verge. Staging MRI confirms tumour confined to the muscularis propria, no lymph node involvement, and 8mm CRM clearance. He has good sphincter function and desires sphincter preservation. He does not wish to have neoadjuvant therapy. Which of the following is the most appropriate operative approach?

options.A: Transanal local excision (TEM or TAMIS) — T2 N0 low rectal cancer can be managed with local excision alone
options.B: Abdominoperineal resection — all T2 cancers at 3cm from the anal verge require APR
options.C: Intersphincteric resection (ISR) with coloanal anastomosis — sphincter-preserving surgery achieving ≥5mm distal margin with curative intent
options.D: Total mesorectal excision via laparoscopic low anterior resection with straight stapling — always feasible for T2 tumours

explanation.correct: For a T2 N0 low rectal cancer at 3cm from the anal verge in a patient desiring sphincter preservation, intersphincteric resection (ISR) is the appropriate sphincter-preserving technique. ISR removes the internal sphincter (and mesorectum) en-bloc with the tumour, achieving adequate circumferential and distal margins while preserving the external sphincter and puborectalis muscle. A coloanal anastomosis is fashioned to the level of the dentate line. ISR is appropriate for T2 and selected T3 tumours where the external sphincter is not involved. A ≥5mm distal margin is achievable. A defunctioning loop ileostomy is fashioned routinely.

wrong.A: Transanal local excision (TEM/TAMIS) alone is oncologically inadequate for T2 rectal cancer. Local excision does not remove the mesorectum or regional lymph nodes, and residual disease risk is high. The local recurrence rate after local excision of T2 tumours is 20–30% — unacceptable in a fit patient. Local excision is appropriate for T1 N0 tumours with favourable histology only.
wrong.B: APR should be reserved for tumours where the external sphincter or levator ani is involved, or where an adequate distal margin cannot be achieved with ISR. With a 3cm distance from the anal verge and a T2 tumour not invading the external sphincter, ISR is feasible and preferable to APR given the patient's desire for sphincter preservation.
wrong.D: A standard low anterior resection with stapled anastomosis at 3cm from the anal verge would require the anastomosis at or below the dentate line — a stapled colorectal anastomosis is technically not possible at 3cm with standard instruments. ISR with handsewn coloanal anastomosis is the appropriate technique for tumours this close to the anal verge.

explanation.guideline: NCCN Rectal Cancer v2.2024 — intersphincteric resection with coloanal anastomosis for low rectal tumours not invading the external sphincter, as an alternative to APR when sphincter preservation is desired; defunctioning ileostomy recommended
explanation.trial: Rullier E et al. (J Am Coll Surg 2005): ISR for low rectal cancers — R0 resection 96%, local recurrence 9% at 5 years, continence adequate in 75% of patients. Martin ST (Ann Surg 2012): ISR vs APR in low rectal cancer — equivalent oncological outcomes with significantly improved quality of life (body image, sexuality).
explanation.pearl: Functional outcomes after ISR: warn patients that continence is impaired compared to standard LAR. Low anterior resection syndrome (LARS) — frequency, urgency, incontinence — affects up to 75% of ISR patients at 1 year, improving to 50% at 5 years. Pre-operative counselling and post-operative biofeedback pelvic floor physiotherapy are essential components of care.
